FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, first wave (Release 1.0), 9-10 year olds (N=3847 after QC), nationally representative sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (average minutes per week on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device) — qualifying smartphone/screen variable; B8 applies: penalized regression (LASSO/Ridge/Elastic net) with traditional regression coefficients reported in main text and figures

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple machine learning regression approaches (LASSO, Ridge, Elastic net, Linear regression) with 10-fold cross-validation; coefficients and model performance metrics reported; F-statistics and R² values provided

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (β weights) shown in Figs. 1-3; RMSE, R² values (training R²=0.12, test R²=0.12 for full sample); p-values for linear regression (e.g., screen time p=7.5×10⁻⁸); FDR-corrected results in Supplementary Fig. S1

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis examining relative importance of 43 demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive variables in predicting BMI using machine learning

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate; first published online July 10, 2019

FT_EC7_reason: Condition (a) fails: Screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable (IV) predicting BMI as the dependent variable (DV), not vice versa. Paper contains SM→non-SM-outcome analyses, violating FT-EC7 requirement that SM/smartphone must be DEPENDENT in every analysis
notes: B8 (ML rule): Penalized regression (LASSO/Ridge/Elastic net) with traditional regression coefficients counted as extractable numeric evidence. Study uses machine learning but reports interpretable β weights and variable importance coefficients.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (Release 2.0), baseline cross-sectional assessment, 9-10 year old children (N=11,007 recruited across 21 US study sites); analyzed sample N=10,666-10,876 depending on variables

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen media with separate coefficients: TV, video, video game use, social media, texting, video chat all reported with independent associations in Tables 2-3 (weekday and weekend use) — B1 applies: YSTS paper with separate β/OR for multiple screen/SM variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression predicting sleep-wake disturbance severity (binary/categorical outcomes); Tables 2-3 report odds ratios (ORs) with 95% CIs and p-values; Cohen's d effect sizes calculated and reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios (ORs) with 95% CIs, p-values (<.001 significance indicators), and Cohen's d effect sizes in Tables 2-3 (both weekday and weekend analyses); relative risk of insufficient sleep reported in Table 4

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis examining associations between different types of screen media use (TV, video, video game, social media, texting, video chat) and symptom severity of sleep-wake disturbances

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; preregistered study (noted in abstract); first published online July 3, 2020

FT_EC7_reason: Condition (a) fails: Screen media is the INDEPENDENT variable (IV) predicting sleep outcomes (non-SM dependent variables), not the reverse. Paper has screen-media→sleep analyses, which violates FT-EC7 requirement that SM/smartphone must be DEPENDENT in every analysis involving SM
notes: B1 applies: Paper reports separate coefficients (ORs) for multiple screen media types (TV, video, video games, social media, texting, video chat) predicting multiple sleep outcomes, meeting B1 criterion for YSTS papers with per-item breakdown of screen/SM variables
